title: Land Registration Fees and Process in Bangladesh: Complete Guide 2025

heroIntro: Land registration in Bangladesh is a multi-step process involving stamp duty, registration fees, and visits to the Sub-Registrar office. Understanding the costs and steps in advance can save you significant money and prevent fraud.
toc: What is Land Registration in Bangladesh?
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Why You Need a Property Lawyer

sections.h2: What is Land Registration in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
Land registration is the formal recording of property ownership with the government through the Sub-Registrar's office under the Registration Act 1908. Without a registered deed, a property transaction is invalid and unenforceable. Registration transfers legal title from seller to buyer and creates a permanent public record. This applies whether you are an individual or registering a real estate company structure. It is also crucial when dividing joint property after marriage dissolution. For major disputes, challenging illegal land acquisition in court might be necessary.

An unregistered sale is one of the most common causes of land disputes in Bangladesh. Always register with the help of a qualified land and property lawyer.

The land registration process in Bangladesh involves these steps:

  1. Document verification: Verify the seller's title chain (CS/SA/RS khatian, mutation records, previous deeds).
  2. Draft the sale deed: A lawyer prepares a deed of sale (baina dil) with all legal particulars.
  3. Purchase stamp paper: Buy non-judicial stamp paper worth the required stamp duty from a licensed vendor.
  4. Visit the Sub-Registrar office: Both buyer and seller (or authorised agents with Power of Attorney) appear with two witnesses.
  5. Biometric verification: Fingerprints of all parties are recorded.
  6. Pay registration fee: Pay at the designated bank or through online gateway.
  7. Deed is registered and returned: The Sub-Registrar registers the deed and returns the certified copy.
  8. Mutation (Namjari): Apply for namjari to update land records in your name at the Assistant Commissioner (Land) office.

Stamp duty on land registration is a major cost. Current rates (subject to annual Finance Act changes):

  • Urban areas (City Corporation): 4% of the deed value
  • Municipal areas (Pourashava): 3% of deed value
  • Rural areas: 2% of deed value

Stamp duty is calculated on the registered deed value, which must equal or exceed the government-assessed Mouza rate. Undervaluing to reduce stamp duty is illegal and can void the registration.

In addition to stamp duty, you pay:

  • Registration fee: 1% of deed value (capped at BDT 30,000 for most transactions)
  • Local government tax: Up to 2% depending on location
  • E-fees: BDT 100–500 for digital processing
  • Certified copy fee: BDT varies

Total additional taxes can bring the effective rate to 6–9% of deed value in city areas. Budget accordingly.

Bring to the Sub-Registrar office:

  • Original seller's deed chain (all previous registered deeds)
  • CS/SA/RS/BS khatian copies
  • Mutation (namjari) records
  • Mouza map excerpt
  • NID cards of buyer, seller, and two witnesses
  • Passport photos of all parties
  • Tax clearance certificate (where applicable)
  • Stamp paper (pre-purchased in required denomination)

sections.h2: Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
sections.content:
Common errors that create legal problems later:

  • Not verifying the title chain: Always trace ownership back at least 25 years.
  • Buying from an unauthorised seller: Verify the seller's Power of Attorney if they are not the named owner.
  • Undervaluing the deed: Illegal and can be challenged later by tax authorities.
  • Skipping mutation: Without namjari, government records still show the old owner.
  • Not checking for encumbrances: Search for any mortgage, attachment, or caveat on the property.

Contact Advocate Md. Shah Alam in Uttara for a complete due diligence check before any property transaction.

faqs.question: How long does land registration take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The actual registration at the Sub-Registrar typically takes 1–3 days once all documents are ready and fees paid. Mutation (namjari) after registration can take 2–6 months.

faqs.question: Can I register land without a lawyer in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Technically yes, but it is inadvisable. A lawyer verifies the title, drafts the deed correctly, and ensures all requirements are met — preventing costly problems later.

faqs.question: What is the Mouza rate in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The Mouza rate is the government-assessed minimum value per decimal (shotangsha) of land in a specific area. Your deed must state at least this value. Rates are updated periodically by the government.

faqs.question: What happens if land is sold without registration?
faqs.answer: An unregistered sale deed is inadmissible as evidence of title in any court and is legally void. The seller can sell the same land again to someone else who registers — and the registered buyer will prevail.

faqs.question: Can NRBs (Non-Resident Bangladeshis) register land in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. An NRB can register land through a Power of Attorney given to a trusted person in Bangladesh, which must be notarised and apostilled in the country of residence, then attested at the Bangladesh Embassy.
